summary: Fourth king of Korea's Joseon Dynasty (r. 1418-1450), remembered as the only Korean ruler besides the Goguryeo king Gwanggaeto to bear the epithet "the Great." He is most famous for overseeing the creation of Hangul, a 28-character alphabet designed to be easy for commoners to learn, through scholars in his Hall of Worthies. His reign also saw major scientific advances led by the low-born inventor Jang Yeong-sil, social reforms including public opinion polls and parental leave for the enslaved nobi class, and military campaigns against Tsushima pirates and the Jurchen.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Sejong the Great (born Yi Do, also known by his princely title Grand Prince Chungnyeong) was the fourth king of Korea's Joseon Dynasty, reigning from 1418 to 1450. He is remembered above all as the creator of the Hangul alphabet and is typically identified in giveaways simply as the "great" Joseon king who introduced Hangul.

clues.clue: Fourth king of the Joseon Dynasty, also called Grand Prince Chungnyeong or Yi Do
examples: name this ruler who introduced the Hangul script, a "great" Joseon king.
Name this fourth Joseon monarch, who chartered the Hall of Worthies and created the Hangul writing system.

clues.clue: Third son of Taejong; his second son Sejo later usurped the throne from his grandson Danjong
examples: This Choson ruler, the only one known as "the Great," promulgated the usage of the Hangul alphabet. This third son of Taejong also widely promoted Confucianism and initially suppressed Buddhism but later tolerated it.

works.name: Hangul and the Hall of Worthies
works.description: Sejong's signature achievement was overseeing the creation of Hangul, a 28-character alphabet meant to replace the Chinese-derived hanja characters and be simple enough that, as the famous saying went, "a wise man could learn it in a morning, and a stupid one in ten days." The project was carried out by scholars in his Hall of Worthies and explained in the manual Hunminjeongeum ("The Proper Sounds for the Instruction of the People"). Sejong also commissioned Song of the Dragons Flying to Heaven, a 125-canto epic glorifying his royal ancestors and one of the first major works written in the new script.

clues.clue: Scholars in his Hall of Worthies produced the Hunminjeongeum manual, promulgating a new alphabet meant to replace Chinese-derived hanja characters and improve commoner literacy
examples: A group of scholars from this ruler's Hall of Worthies produced the Hunminjeongeum, which aimed to improve peasant literacy by introducing a new alphabet.
This institution of scholars, established by Sejong, is credited with the invention of Hangul.

clues.clue: The new script was designed so "a wise man could learn it in a morning, and a stupid one in ten days"
examples: This ruler presided over the creation of a system a "wise man" could learn in a morning and a "stupid" one in ten days with the help of his Hall of Worthies.
It was said that an innovation from this monarch's reign could be learnt by wise men in a morning and by stupid men in ten days.

clues.clue: Created a 28-character alphabet designed for ease of learning, partially replacing hanja
examples: This ruler created a 28-character alphabet designed for ease of learning, which partially replaced Chinese-derived hanja.

clues.clue: Commissioned the Song of the Dragons Flying to Heaven, a 125-canto epic glorifying six royal ancestors
examples: This ruler ordered the compilation of a 125-canto epic glorifying six ancestors titled Song of the Dragons Flying to Heaven.
A poetry collection compiled by this man includes an entry detailing how trees with deep roots don't sway in the wind, and is entitled The Songs of the Dragons Flying to Heaven.

works.name: Jang Yeong-sil and Scientific Inventions
works.description: Sejong sponsored major scientific advances led by Jang Yeong-sil, a scientist of low-born status whom Sejong elevated despite class barriers. Jang invented the Cheugugi, an early standardized rain gauge measuring precipitation in "poon" units, and improved on Choe Yun-ui's earlier work to create the Gabinja, an advanced bronze movable-type printing press. Jang fell from royal favor after a sedan chair he built for Sejong broke.

clues.clue: Jang Yeong-sil, a scientist elevated from low-born status, invented the Cheugugi, an early standardized bronze rain gauge measuring precipitation in "poon" units
examples: This ruler sponsored the inventor of a bronze rain gauge despite that inventor's membership in his kingdom's lower class.
Another of Jang's inventions was the Cheugugi, a device for measuring this phenomenon in poon, units of roughly an eighth of an inch.

clues.clue: Jang Yeong-sil improved on Choe Yun-ui's earlier work to create the Gabinja, an advanced bronze movable-type printing press
examples: Jang improved on the pioneering efforts of Choe Yun-ui to produce the Gabinja, an improved version of this device. Another one of these device was created by Jang's rough contemporary, Johann Gutenberg.
On the death of his mother, this ruler commissioned an early translation of the Lotus Sutra using gabinja movable type.

clues.clue: Jang fell from royal favor after a sedan chair he built for Sejong broke while transporting him
examples: Jang fell out of royal favor when a sedan chair he designed broke while transporting this monarch, his patron, the creator of the Hall of Worthies.
though his downfall came when he provided the king with a faulty sedan chair.

clues.clue: Reformed the country's map system to place the capital at the center, improving astronomical predictions
examples: As part of his initiative to advance science in his country, this ruler reformed his country's map system to place his capital at the center, leading to more accurate astronomical predictions.

works.name: Social and Economic Reforms
works.description: Sejong pursued unusually consultative social and economic policies for his era. He ran a five-month public opinion poll surveying roughly 17,000 people before implementing a new agricultural tax, and granted extended parental (maternity and paternity) leave to members of the enslaved nobi class. He also sponsored the agricultural manual Straight Talk on Farming and instituted the Peasants Relocation Policy to resettle newly conquered northern territory.

clues.clue: Ran a public opinion poll of roughly 17,000 people over five months before implementing a new agricultural tax
examples: This ruler surveyed approximately 17,000 people to assess public opinion of a new tax on farmers, as recorded in the fourth section of the Veritable Records of this ruler's dynasty.
This ruler launched early examples of public opinion polls about his policies affecting the nobi class.

clues.clue: Granted extended maternity and paternity leave to members of the enslaved nobi class
examples: This ruler, who granted parental leave to a serf class called nobi, said a wise man could learn of his innovations in a morning, while a fool could learn it in a week.
In another reform, this ruler granted members of the enslaved nobi class extended maternity and paternity leave.

clues.clue: Sponsored the agricultural manual Straight Talk on Farming, which accompanied a push for standardized rain gauges
examples: The treatise Straight Talk on Farming accompanied this ruler's push for agriculture reform that included the first standardized rain gauge.
This monarch's reign saw the publication and widespread distribution of the agricultural manual called the Straight Talk on Farming.

clues.clue: Instituted the "Peasants Relocation Policy" (samin) to resettle newly conquered northern territory near the Yalu River
examples: This ruler instituted the "Peasants Relocation Policy" to attract people to newly conquered northern areas of his kingdom.

clues.clue: His Stabilization Office introduced a cash coin to replace officially stamped cotton currency
examples: This ruler's Stabilization Office introduced a cash coin to replace officially stamped cotton.

works.name: Military Campaigns: the Oei Invasion and the Jurchen Frontier
works.description: Sejong launched the Oei Invasion (also called the Gihae Eastern Expedition) of 1419 against Japanese wokou pirates based on Tsushima Island, controlled by the So clan; the campaign, launched on his father Taejong's orders, later secured the So clan trading privileges under the Kakitsu Treaty. He also established a series of four forts and six outposts along the northern frontier to counter incursions by the Jurchen.

clues.clue: Launched the Oei Invasion (Gihae Eastern Expedition) of 1419 against wokou pirates on Tsushima Island, controlled by the So clan, on his father Taejong's orders
examples: The Oei invasion against pirates on Tsushima was launched by this ruler, who assembled scholars in the Hall of Worthies to develop a block-based script.
Under the guidance of his father, this man launched an expedition against a polity controlled by So Sadashige that had become a base for pirates; that assault on Tsushima was the Oei invasion.

clues.clue: After the Oei Invasion, granted the So family trading privileges under the Kakitsu Treaty
examples: This monarch granted the So family trading privileges in the Kakitsu Treaty, after the Oei Invasion, a raid on pirate bases on Tsushima, took place during his reign.

clues.clue: Established a series of four forts and six military outposts to counteract the Jurchen
examples: This ruler established a series of four forts and six military outposts to counteract the Jurchen.

works.name: Succession Crisis: the Six Martyred Ministers
works.description: Sejong's second son, Sejo, later staged a coup against Sejong's grandson (and Sejo's own nephew), King Danjong. Opposition to that coup was led by a group of scholars known as the Six Martyred Ministers, several of whom had originally been appointed by Sejong to his Hall of Worthies; Sejo executed them and disbanded the institution after seizing power.

clues.clue: Sejong's second son, Sejo, staged a coup against Sejong's grandson Danjong; the Six Martyred Ministers, originally appointed by Sejong, opposed the coup and were executed
examples: A research institute set up by this ruler was disbanded after the Six Martyred Ministers plotted to overthrow a later ruler.
This man's second son was targeted in a coup attempt by the Six Martyred Ministers, who were originally appointed by this man.

clues.clue: Banished two prospective princesses for his heir, one accused of witchcraft and the other of having an affair with a lowborn maid
examples: This ruler banished two prospective princesses for his heir, one for being a witch and the other for having a lesbian affair with a lowborn maid.

works.name: Legacy: Memory and Cultural Depictions
works.description: Sejong is honored throughout modern South Korea: a massive statue of him stands next to Gyeongbokgung Palace in Seoul, an Aegis-enabled KDX Phase III class of naval destroyers bears his name, and a modern think tank was named after him following the failed Rangoon Bombing. Along with Gwanggaeto of Goguryeo, he is one of only two Korean rulers to carry the epithet "the Great."

clues.clue: A massive statue of him stands next to Gyeongbokgung Palace in Seoul
examples: A massive statue of this ruler was built next to his Gyeongbokgung Palace.

clues.clue: Lends his name to an Aegis-enabled KDX Phase III class of naval destroyers
examples: This ruler lends his name to an Aegis-enabled KDX Phase III class of destroyers.

clues.clue: A modern think tank was named after him following the failed Rangoon Bombing
examples: A modern think tank named after this ruler was set up after the unsuccessful Rangoon Bombing.

works.name: Religious Policy
works.description: Sejong actively promoted Confucianism as the state ideology and initially suppressed Buddhism before later tolerating it, even commissioning an early translation of the Lotus Sutra using Gabinja movable type after his mother's death. He also issued a decree banning the practice of Islam among his realm's predominantly Turkic "huihui" community.

clues.clue: Promoted Confucianism as state ideology; initially suppressed Buddhism but later tolerated it
examples: This Choson ruler ... also widely promoted Confucianism and initially suppressed Buddhism but later tolerated it.

clues.clue: Issued a decree banning the practice of Islam by his realm's predominantly Turkic "huihui" community
examples: This ruler issued a decree banning the practice of Islam by his realm's predominantly Turkic "huihui" community.

comprehensive_summary: Sejong the Great (born Yi Do, also Grand Prince Chungnyeong), the third son of Taejong, ruled as the fourth king of Korea's Joseon Dynasty from 1418 to 1450 and is one of only two Korean rulers -- alongside the Goguryeo king Gwanggaeto -- to carry the epithet "the Great." His signature achievement was overseeing the creation of Hangul, a 28-character alphabet designed so that, per the famous saying, "a wise man could learn it in a morning, and a stupid one in ten days"; the project was carried out by scholars in his Hall of Worthies and explained in the Hunminjeongeum manual, and he commissioned the Song of the Dragons Flying to Heaven, a 125-canto epic in the new script glorifying his ancestors. His reign was also a golden age of science under the low-born scientist Jang Yeong-sil, whom Sejong elevated despite class barriers; Jang built the Cheugugi rain gauge and the Gabinja printing press (an improvement on Choe Yun-ui's earlier device) before falling from favor when a sedan chair he built for the king broke. Sejong pursued notably consultative social policy, surveying roughly 17,000 people over five months before enacting a new agricultural tax, granting parental leave to the enslaved nobi class, sponsoring the farming manual Straight Talk on Farming, instituting the Peasants Relocation Policy to resettle the northern frontier, and reforming currency through his Stabilization Office. Militarily, he launched the 1419 Oei Invasion (Gihae Eastern Expedition) against wokou pirates on Tsushima, later granting the So clan trading rights under the Kakitsu Treaty, and built four forts and six outposts against the Jurchen. He promoted Confucianism while alternately suppressing and tolerating Buddhism, and banned the practice of Islam among the Turkic "huihui" community. Late in his reign, his second son Sejo's eventual coup against Sejong's grandson Danjong was opposed by the Six Martyred Ministers, several of them former Hall of Worthies scholars whom Sejo executed after seizing the throne. Today Sejong is commemorated by a statue beside Gyeongbokgung Palace, the KDX Phase III Aegis destroyer class, and a think tank founded after the failed Rangoon Bombing.
recursive_suggestions: Jang Yeong-sil -- Sejong's low-born chief scientist; independently clued with the Cheugugi rain gauge and Gabinja printing press
Joseon Dynasty -- broader dynastic topic distinct from Sejong's individual biography; clued separately with founding by Taejo, the Imjin War, and later decline
Sejo of Joseon -- Sejong's second son, whose coup against Danjong and purge of the Six Martyred Ministers could support a standalone figure page
Gwanggaeto the Great -- the only other Korean ruler with the "the Great" epithet; distinct Goguryeo-era figure
Hangul -- the writing system itself could be treated as a standalone linguistic/cultural topic
